reasoning_model_1: Model 1 fails to execute the command correctly regarding the paralinguistic feature. Instead of performing a non-linguistic yawn sound, the model literally speaks the word 'Yawns' at the beginning of the audio (00:00-00:01). This is a hallucination where the model reads the intended action as part of the script. Consequently, it fails the core requirement of generating a 'Non-Linguistic Vocalization'. While the subsequent delivery of 'This lecture is so boring...' (00:01-00:05) has a somewhat weary tone, the addition of the spoken word 'Yawns' and the absence of an actual yawn sound effect constitutes a major error in both content accuracy and paralinguistic compliance.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 successfully executes the command. At the very beginning (00:00-00:01), it produces a clear, natural-sounding non-linguistic vocalization that resembles a yawn or a deep, bored sigh ('Ahhh'). Following this, it reads the script 'This lecture is so boring, it makes me yawn' (00:01-00:06) with high fidelity and a tone appropriate for the context (boredom). The integration of the yawn is natural and does not interfere with the clarity of the speech. The model accurately distinguishes between the instruction to yawn and the text to be spoken.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner. The instruction required the generation of a non-linguistic vocalization (a yawn). Model 2 correctly interpreted this and produced the sound of a yawn before speaking the text. Model 1, conversely, failed to distinguish between the instruction and the text, literally speaking the word 'Yawns' instead of making the sound. This represents a failure in understanding the paralinguistic control task. Model 2 demonstrates superior control over non-linguistic features and better content accuracy by not adding unprompted words.
